question:
Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a nonempty collection of subsets of $\{1,\dots, n\}$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
if $S, S' \in \mathcal{P}$, then $S \cup S' \in \mathcal{P}$ and $S \cap S' \in \mathcal{P}$, and
\item[(ii)]
if $S \in \mathcal{P}$ and $S \neq \emptyset$, then there is a subset $T \subset S$
such that $T \in \mathcal{P}$ and $T$ contains exactly one fewer element than $S$.
\end{enumerate}
Suppose that $f: \mathcal{P} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a function such that
$f(\emptyset) = 0$ and
\[
f(S \cup S') = f(S) + f(S') - f(S \cap S') \mbox{ for all $S,S' \in \mathcal{P}$.}
\]
Must there exist real numbers $f_1,\dots,f_n$ such that
\[
f(S) = \sum_{i \in S} f_i
\]
for every $S \in \mathcal{P}$?

descriptive_long.question:
Let $setfamily$ be a nonempty collection of subsets of $\{1,\dots,setsize\}$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
if $subseta, subseta' \in setfamily$, then $subseta \cup subseta' \in setfamily$ and $subseta \cap subseta' \in setfamily$, and
\item[(ii)]
if $subseta \in setfamily$ and $subseta \neq \emptyset$, then there is a subset $subsetb \subset subseta$ such that $subsetb \in setfamily$ and $subsetb$ contains exactly one fewer element than $subseta$.
\end{enumerate}
Suppose that $function: setfamily \to \mathbb{R}$ is a function such that $function(\emptyset) = 0$ and
\[
function(subseta \cup subseta') = function(subseta) + function(subseta') - function(subseta \cap subseta') \mbox{ for all $subseta, subseta' \in setfamily$.}
\]
Must there exist real numbers $functionone,\dots,functionend$ such that
\[
function(subseta) = \sum_{indexone \in subseta} functionvar
\]
for every $subseta \in setfamily$?

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $\mathcal{lanternfish}$ be a nonempty collection of subsets of $\{1,\dots, paintbrush\}$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
if $pineapple, pineapple' \in \mathcal{lanternfish}$, then $pineapple \cup pineapple' \in \mathcal{lanternfish}$ and $pineapple \cap pineapple' \in \mathcal{lanternfish}$, and
\item[(ii)]
if $pineapple \in \mathcal{lanternfish}$ and $pineapple \neq \emptyset$, then there is a subset $raindrop \subset pineapple$
such that $raindrop \in \mathcal{lanternfish}$ and $raindrop$ contains exactly one fewer element than $pineapple$.
\end{enumerate}
Suppose that $storyboard: \mathcal{lanternfish} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a function such that
$storyboard(\emptyset) = 0$ and
\[
storyboard(pineapple \cup pineapple') = storyboard(pineapple) + storyboard(pineapple') - storyboard(pineapple \cap pineapple') \mbox{ for all $pineapple,pineapple' \in \mathcal{lanternfish}$.}
\]
Must there exist real numbers $gemstone,\dots,cornfield$ such that
\[
storyboard(pineapple) = \sum_{squirrel \in pineapple} sandcastle
\]
for every $pineapple \in \mathcal{lanternfish}$?

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $\mathcal{singularset}$ be a nonempty collection of subsets of $\{1,\dots, infinite\}$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
if $superset, superset' \in \mathcal{singularset}$, then $superset \cup superset' \in \mathcal{singularset}$ and $superset \cap superset' \in \mathcal{singularset}$, and
\item[(ii)]
if $superset \in \mathcal{singularset}$ and $superset \neq \emptyset$, then there is a subset $totalset \subset superset$
such that $totalset \in \mathcal{singularset}$ and $totalset$ contains exactly one fewer element than $superset$.
\end{enumerate}
Suppose that $constant: \mathcal{singularset} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a function such that
$constant(\emptyset) = 0$ and
\[
constant(superset \cup superset') = constant(superset) + constant(superset') - constant(superset \cap superset') \mbox{ for all $superset,superset' \in \mathcal{singularset}$.}
\]
Must there exist real numbers $variableone,\dots,variablelast$ such that
\[
constant(superset) = \sum_{summation \in superset} variable
\]
for every $superset \in \mathcal{singularset}$?

garbled_string.question:
Let $\mathcal{kusdqnwz}$ be a nonempty collection of subsets of $\{1,\dots, qwertyui\}$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
if $qzxwvtnp, qzxwvtnp' \in \mathcal{kusdqnwz}$, then $qzxwvtnp \cup qzxwvtnp' \in \mathcal{kusdqnwz}$ and $qzxwvtnp \cap qzxwvtnp' \in \mathcal{kusdqnwz}$, and
\item[(ii)]
if $qzxwvtnp \in \mathcal{kusdqnwz}$ and $qzxwvtnp \neq \emptyset$, then there is a subset $hjgrksla \subset qzxwvtnp$
such that $hjgrksla \in \mathcal{kusdqnwz}$ and $hjgrksla$ contains exactly one fewer element than $qzxwvtnp$.
\end{enumerate}
Suppose that $zxcvbnma: \mathcal{kusdqnwz} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a function such that
$zxcvbnma(\emptyset) = 0$ and
\[
zxcvbnma(qzxwvtnp \cup qzxwvtnp') = zxcvbnma(qzxwvtnp) + zxcvbnma(qzxwvtnp') - zxcvbnma(qzxwvtnp \cap qzxwvtnp') \mbox{ for all $qzxwvtnp,qzxwvtnp' \in \mathcal{kusdqnwz}$.}
\]
Must there exist real numbers $qazwsxed,\dots,rfvtgbyh$ such that
\[
zxcvbnma(qzxwvtnp) = \sum_{kljhgfds \in qzxwvtnp} plmoknji
\]
for every $qzxwvtnp \in \mathcal{kusdqnwz}$?

kernel_variant.question:
Let m be a positive integer and put
V = {v_1 , v_2 , \dots , v_m} .
Let \Pi  be a non-empty family of subsets of V that satisfies
(i)  (closure) whenever A , B \in  \Pi  we also have A \cup  B \in  \Pi  and A \cap  B \in  \Pi  ;
(ii) (peeling) whenever A \in  \Pi  is non-empty there exists B \subset  A with |B| = |A| - 1 and B \in  \Pi  .
Let
F : \Pi  \to  Q[t]
be a map into the additive group of polynomials with rational coefficients such that
F(\emptyset ) = 0 and F(A \cup  B) = F(A) + F(B) - F(A \cap  B) (for all A , B \in  \Pi ).
Show that there exist polynomials P_1 (t) , \ldots  , P_m (t) \in  Q[t] satisfying
F(A) = \sum _{v_i \in  A} P_i (t) for every A \in  \Pi  .
kernel_variant.solution:
We prove that suitable polynomials P_1 (t), \ldots  , P_m (t) always exist.

1.  Two preliminary lemmas

Lemma 1 (minimal singleton decomposition).
Fix an index j for which v_j occurs in at least one member of \Pi .  There are sets S , T \in  \Pi  with
S = T \cup  {v_j} and T \cap  {v_j} = \emptyset  .
Proof.
Choose S_0 \in  \Pi  containing v_j of minimal cardinality.  By (ii) there is a subset T \subset  S_0 with |T| = |S_0| - 1 and T \in  \Pi .  If T still contained v_j the minimality of S_0 would be contradicted, hence S_0 \ T = {v_j}.  Put S = S_0. \blacksquare 

Lemma 2 (difference independence).
Suppose S_1 , T_1 , S_2 , T_2 \in  \Pi  satisfy for some fixed j
S_1 = T_1 \cup  {v_j}, T_1 \cap  {v_j} = \emptyset , S_2 = T_2 \cup  {v_j}, T_2 \cap  {v_j} = \emptyset .
Then   F(S_1) - F(T_1) = F(S_2) - F(T_2).
Proof.
Let U = T_1 \cup  T_2.  Because v_j \notin  T_1 \cup  T_2 we have S_1 \cap  T_2 = T_1 \cap  T_2 and T_1 \cap  S_2 = T_1 \cap  T_2.  Apply the inclusion-exclusion identity with pairs (S_1 , T_2) and (T_1 , S_2):
F(S_1 \cup  T_2) = F(S_1) + F(T_2) - F(T_1 \cap  T_2),
F(T_1 \cup  S_2) = F(T_1) + F(S_2) - F(T_1 \cap  T_2).
But S_1 \cup  T_2 = T_1 \cup  S_2 = U \cup  {v_j}, so the left-hand sides agree.  Cancelling F(T_1 \cap  T_2) yields the desired equality. \blacksquare 

2.  Definition of the coefficients P_j
For each j = 1 , \ldots  , m define
P_j (t) =
  {  F(S) - F(T)   if v_j belongs to some member of \Pi , where S , T are as in Lemma 1,
     0               otherwise.
  }
Lemma 2 shows that the right-hand side is independent of the particular choice of S and T, so P_j is well-defined.

3.  Verification that F(A) decomposes as required
We prove by induction on k = |A| that for every A \in  \Pi ,
(*) F(A) = \sum _{v_i \in  A} P_i (t).

Base case k = 0.
Property (ii) applied repeatedly to any set in \Pi  shows that \emptyset  \in  \Pi .  The hypothesis gives F(\emptyset ) = 0 while the sum on the right-hand side is empty, hence 0; so (*) holds.

Induction step.
Assume (*) holds for all sets in \Pi  of size strictly smaller than k \geq  1.  Fix A \in  \Pi  with |A| = k.  By (ii) there exists B \subset  A with |B| = k - 1 and B \in  \Pi .  Choose v_j \in  A \ B so that A = B \cup  {v_j}; then B \cap  {v_j} = \emptyset .

Because A and B have exactly the relation required in Lemma 2, that lemma together with the definition of P_j gives
F(A) - F(B) = P_j (t).
Hence
F(A) = F(B) + P_j (t).
By the induction hypothesis F(B) = \sum _{v_i \in  B} P_i (t), so
F(A) = \sum _{v_i \in  B} P_i (t) + P_j (t) = \sum _{v_i \in  A} P_i (t).
Thus (*) is true for every A with |A| = k, completing the induction.

Consequently the polynomials P_1 (t), \ldots  , P_m (t) satisfy F(A) = \sum _{v_i \in  A} P_i (t) for all A \in  \Pi , as required. \blacksquare

core_steps: Minimal-element argument: for any i that appears somewhere, find S,T∈𝓟 with S = T ∪ {i} and T∩{i}=∅ (uses property (ii)).
Difference-independence lemma: show f(S) − f(T) depends only on i, not on the chosen S,T (uses union/intersection closure plus additivity identity).
Define f_i for each i as that common difference (or 0 if i never occurs).
Induct on |S|, peeling off one element with property (ii) to prove f(S)=Σ_{i∈S} f_i.

slot1.description: Size and symbols of the ground set; any finite set of cardinality n works.

slot2.description: Target space of the function; any abelian group supporting + and − suffices.
